chunks.title: What is AI
chunks.text: Artificial intelligence is the study of building systems that perform tasks usually requiring human intelligence — perception, reasoning, learning, planning, language. Narrow AI handles specific tasks (chess, translation, image recognition); general AI matches human ability across domains and remains aspirational. Modern AI is dominated by machine learning, where systems learn patterns from data rather than being explicitly programmed.

chunks.title: Search algorithms
chunks.text: Many AI problems reduce to searching a state space for a path from start to goal. Uninformed search: BFS finds shortest path by steps, DFS uses less memory, iterative deepening combines them. Informed (heuristic) search uses an estimate of remaining cost. Greedy best-first expands the node closest to the goal by heuristic. A* combines path cost so far with heuristic estimate — optimal when the heuristic is admissible (never overestimates).

chunks.title: Knowledge representation
chunks.text: AI systems represent facts in a form that supports reasoning. Propositional logic handles simple true/false facts; first-order logic adds variables, predicates, and quantifiers (∀, ∃) for richer statements. Semantic networks and ontologies capture relationships between concepts. Probabilistic representations (Bayesian networks) handle uncertainty by modelling joint distributions over variables compactly via conditional independence.

chunks.title: Planning
chunks.text: Planning is finding a sequence of actions that transforms an initial state into a goal state. STRIPS represents actions with preconditions and effects (add/delete lists). Forward search explores from the initial state; backward search from the goal. Heuristic planners use relaxation (ignore some constraints) to estimate distance to goal. Partial-order planning leaves action ordering flexible until forced. Real-world planners power robotics, logistics, and game NPCs.

chunks.title: Machine learning fundamentals
chunks.text: Machine learning systems improve from experience. Supervised learning maps inputs to known outputs (regression, classification). Unsupervised learning finds structure in unlabeled data (clustering, dimensionality reduction). Reinforcement learning learns a policy by trial and error with reward signals. Modern deep learning uses neural networks with many layers to learn hierarchical features, powering vision, language, and speech systems.

chunks.title: Natural language processing
chunks.text: NLP gives computers the ability to read, understand, and generate human language. Classical pipelines combined tokenization, POS tagging, parsing, named entity recognition, and dependency analysis. Modern NLP is dominated by transformer-based language models that learn token-level representations from massive text corpora. Tasks: translation, summarization, question answering, sentiment analysis, named entity recognition, and chatbot conversation.

chunks.title: Computer vision
chunks.text: Computer vision systems interpret images and video. Classical pipelines use edge detection, feature extraction (SIFT, ORB), and matching. Modern systems use convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and vision transformers, trained on labeled image datasets like ImageNet. Tasks include image classification, object detection (YOLO, Faster R-CNN), semantic segmentation (predict a class per pixel), and instance segmentation. Self-driving cars, medical imaging, and AR all depend on it.

chunks.title: AI ethics and bias
chunks.text: ML systems amplify biases present in their training data. Recidivism prediction systems have shown racial bias; hiring screeners have downranked women's resumes; face recognition is less accurate on darker skin tones. Mitigation: audit datasets, evaluate per subgroup, use fairness-aware training. Other concerns: privacy (models can memorize training data), interpretability (deep models are hard to explain), and accountability when an AI makes a harmful decision.

chunks.title: Minimax and game trees
chunks.text: Minimax is the classic algorithm for two-player zero-sum games (chess, tic-tac-toe). The game tree alternates max nodes (your turn, pick the best for you) and min nodes (opponent's turn, picks the worst for you). The algorithm recursively evaluates leaves and propagates values upward. Exhaustive search is exponential; depth-limited search with heuristic evaluation at the cutoff makes it practical.

chunks.title: Alpha-beta pruning
chunks.text: Alpha-beta pruning eliminates branches of the minimax tree that can't affect the final decision. Alpha is the best score the maximizer can guarantee so far; beta is the best the minimizer can guarantee. When alpha >= beta in a subtree, the rest is pruned. With good move ordering, alpha-beta searches twice as deep as plain minimax for the same effort.

chunks.title: Markov Decision Processes
chunks.text: An MDP models sequential decision-making with states, actions, transition probabilities, and rewards. The Markov property: the next state depends only on the current state and action, not the full history. The goal is a policy mapping states to actions that maximizes expected cumulative reward. Solving an MDP exactly uses value iteration or policy iteration; for large MDPs, reinforcement learning approximates the solution.

chunks.title: Q-learning
chunks.text: Q-learning is a model-free reinforcement learning algorithm. It learns the action-value function Q(s, a) — expected return from taking action a in state s and following the optimal policy thereafter. Update rule: Q(s,a) ← Q(s,a) + α [r + γ·max Q(s',a') − Q(s,a)]. Tabular Q-learning works for small state spaces; deep Q-networks (DQN) use a neural network to approximate Q for large state spaces (Atari games).

chunks.title: Policy gradient methods
chunks.text: Policy gradient methods directly optimize the policy (a probability distribution over actions) instead of a value function. The policy is parameterized by a neural network; gradients of expected return with respect to the network weights are estimated from sampled trajectories (REINFORCE algorithm). Better for continuous action spaces (robotics) and high-variance gradients. PPO and TRPO add stability with trust regions.

chunks.title: Actor-critic
chunks.text: Actor-critic combines a policy (actor) with a value function (critic). The actor decides actions; the critic evaluates them and provides a lower-variance gradient signal than pure policy gradient. A2C and A3C are synchronous and asynchronous variants. PPO (proximal policy optimization) is the modern actor-critic default — stable, simple, the workhorse of RLHF in language models.

chunks.title: Multi-armed bandits
chunks.text: A multi-armed bandit is a simplified RL problem: K levers, each pays a stochastic reward, and you have a budget of pulls. Goal: maximise total reward, balancing exploration (sample under-tried levers) and exploitation (pull the best so far). ε-greedy explores with probability ε; UCB picks the lever with the highest upper confidence bound; Thompson sampling samples from posterior distributions. Used in A/B testing and ad placement.

chunks.title: Neural network basics
chunks.text: A feedforward neural network is a sequence of layers, each applying a linear transformation (weights × input + bias) followed by a non-linear activation function. Inputs pass forward; outputs are predictions. Training adjusts weights to minimise a loss function via backpropagation and gradient descent. Hidden layers learn increasingly abstract features; the depth gives 'deep learning' its name.

chunks.title: Backpropagation
chunks.text: Backpropagation efficiently computes gradients of the loss with respect to every weight in a neural network. It uses the chain rule of calculus: gradients are passed backward layer by layer from output to input. Each gradient is the product of the local gradient (how the layer's output depends on its input) and the upstream gradient. Modern frameworks (PyTorch, TensorFlow) automate this via auto-differentiation.

chunks.title: Activation functions
chunks.text: Activation functions introduce non-linearity, letting networks model complex patterns. Sigmoid (smooth, 0 to 1) was historical default but saturates and kills gradients. Tanh is similar but centered at zero. ReLU (max(0, x)) avoids saturation for positive inputs and is the modern default. Variants: Leaky ReLU, GELU (used in transformers), Swish. Softmax produces probabilities over classes for classification outputs.

chunks.title: Loss functions
chunks.text: The loss measures how wrong the model is. Mean squared error (MSE) for regression. Cross-entropy for classification — penalises confident wrong predictions heavily. Hinge loss for SVMs. Triplet loss for embedding learning (anchor closer to positive than negative). KL divergence between predicted and target distributions in language modelling. Choosing the right loss is half the modelling problem.

chunks.title: Gradient descent variants
chunks.text: Batch gradient descent averages gradients over the full dataset — accurate but slow. Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) uses one sample at a time — noisy but fast. Mini-batch SGD strikes a balance (batches of 32-1024). Optimizers add momentum (carry past gradients), adaptive learning rates per parameter (Adam, RMSProp). Adam is the default modern optimizer; SGD with momentum is still preferred for some computer vision tasks.

chunks.title: Convolutional neural networks
chunks.text: CNNs apply learnable filters (kernels) that slide over an image, detecting features. Convolutional layers share weights spatially — translation-invariant, far fewer parameters than fully-connected. Pooling layers downsample. Deep CNNs stack many conv layers learning hierarchical features (edges → textures → parts → objects). Classics: LeNet, AlexNet, VGG, ResNet, EfficientNet. CNNs dominated computer vision before vision transformers.

chunks.title: Recurrent neural networks and LSTMs
chunks.text: RNNs process sequences by maintaining a hidden state updated at each timestep. Vanilla RNNs suffer vanishing/exploding gradients on long sequences. LSTMs (long short-term memory) add input, forget, and output gates that control information flow, enabling much longer dependencies. GRUs are a simpler alternative. LSTMs ruled NLP and speech for a decade before transformers; still used in time-series forecasting.

chunks.title: Generative adversarial networks
chunks.text: A GAN trains two networks competing: a generator G learns to produce realistic samples; a discriminator D learns to distinguish real from generated. Each improves by fooling/catching the other. Trained correctly, G produces samples indistinguishable from the training distribution. Famous applications: StyleGAN (photorealistic faces), CycleGAN (unpaired image translation). GANs are notoriously unstable to train.

chunks.title: Variational autoencoders
chunks.text: A VAE is a generative model that learns a continuous latent space. The encoder maps inputs to a distribution (mean and variance); a sample is drawn and passed through the decoder to reconstruct the input. Training uses reconstruction loss plus a KL divergence term that keeps the latent distribution close to a standard normal. VAEs interpolate smoothly in latent space — great for understanding learned structure.

chunks.title: Diffusion models
chunks.text: Diffusion models generate images by reversing a noising process. Forward process: gradually add Gaussian noise to a real image until it's pure noise. Reverse process: a neural network learns to denoise step by step. At inference, start from random noise and iteratively denoise. Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 3, Midjourney use diffusion. Quality and controllability surpassed GANs around 2022.

chunks.title: Word embeddings
chunks.text: Word embeddings map words to dense vectors capturing semantic similarity — 'king' and 'queen' are close, and 'king − man + woman ≈ queen'. Word2Vec (Mikolov 2013) trains skip-gram or CBOW models to predict context words. GloVe uses co-occurrence statistics. FastText handles morphology. Modern transformer-based embeddings (BERT, sentence-transformers) produce contextual embeddings that vary with usage.

chunks.title: Attention mechanism
chunks.text: Attention lets a model weigh different parts of its input when producing each output. The mechanism computes a weighted sum of value vectors, weights derived from a similarity score between a query and keys. Self-attention has queries, keys, and values all coming from the same sequence, modelling relationships between every pair of positions. Replaced recurrence as the dominant sequence-modelling primitive.

chunks.title: Transformer architecture
chunks.text: The Transformer (Vaswani et al. 2017) stacks layers of multi-head self-attention followed by feedforward networks, with residual connections and layer normalisation. Multi-head attention runs several attention computations in parallel with different projections, capturing different relationship types. Positional encodings inject sequence order since attention itself is permutation-invariant. Foundation of BERT, GPT, T5, every modern LLM.

chunks.title: BERT
chunks.text: BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers, 2018) is a transformer encoder pre-trained on two tasks: masked language modelling (predict masked tokens) and next sentence prediction. The bidirectional context (a token sees both left and right) made it strong for classification, NER, QA. Fine-tuning BERT on a downstream task with a small head became the dominant NLP paradigm 2018-2020 before generative models took over.

chunks.title: GPT family
chunks.text: GPT (Generative Pre-trained Transformer) is a transformer decoder trained autoregressively — predict the next token given all previous tokens. Scaling parameters (GPT-2: 1.5B, GPT-3: 175B, GPT-4: undisclosed but trillions of effective parameters) plus diverse training data and instruction-tuning produced models capable of conversation, reasoning, and code generation. Generative, conversational AI in production today is dominated by GPT-style decoder-only models.

chunks.title: Foundation models
chunks.text: Foundation models are large models pre-trained on broad data, adapted to many downstream tasks via fine-tuning, prompting, or in-context learning. Examples: GPT-4, Claude, LLaMA (text), CLIP (vision-language), Whisper (speech), Stable Diffusion (image). The term emphasises that they form the foundation for an ecosystem of derived models and applications. Risks: centralised power, training data provenance, biases at scale.

chunks.title: RLHF
chunks.text: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback aligns language model outputs with human preferences. Pipeline: collect ranked comparisons of model outputs, train a reward model to predict the rankings, then fine-tune the language model with PPO using the reward model as the reward signal. RLHF turned GPT-3.5 into ChatGPT — same base model, very different behaviour. Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is a simpler alternative skipping the reward-model step.

chunks.title: AI safety and alignment
chunks.text: AI alignment is the problem of making AI systems pursue the goals their designers actually intended. Risks include reward hacking (optimizing the proxy instead of the true goal), deceptive alignment, and capability generalization without value generalization. Current techniques: RLHF, constitutional AI, red-teaming, interpretability research, scalable oversight. Open problems get more urgent as capabilities scale faster than understanding.

chunks.title: Expert systems and symbolic AI
chunks.text: Pre-deep-learning AI relied heavily on expert systems — handcrafted rules elicited from domain experts (medical diagnosis, equipment configuration). MYCIN (1970s) diagnosed blood infections from rules. The knowledge-acquisition bottleneck (experts can't articulate everything) and brittleness on edge cases limited the approach. Symbolic AI is enjoying renewed interest in hybrid systems — neuro-symbolic AI combines learned representations with explicit reasoning.
